content.formatted_body:
Trying to implement the consumer side tests now. What does a generatedPositionFor() return when the regular mapping was a range?

const tracer = new TraceMap({
  version: 3,
  sources: ['input.js'],
  sourceRoot: 'https://astexplorer.net/',
  names: ['foo'],
  mappings: 'AAAA', // (0, 0) -> (0, 0)
  rangeMappings: 'B', // first mapping is range
});

const pos = generatedPositionFor(traceMap, {
  source: 'input.js',
  line: 2,
  column: 2,
});
// ^?

content.body: Do ranges cover original source code until the next mapping position in the original file?
The ranges could be differently sized. Eg, `AAAA,BAee` only covers 0 lines/1 column in generated code, but 15 lines/15 columns in original code.
content.formatted_body: The ranges could be differently sized. Eg, AAAA,BAee only covers 0 lines/1 column in generated code, but 15 lines/15 columns in original code.

content.body:
This is even worse:
```
mappings: 'AAAA,BAee'
rangeMappings: 'B'
```
Doing `generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 10 })` would return `{ line: 1, column: 10 }` offset from the range mapping, which is beyond the start of the `BAee` segement.
content.formatted_body: Doing generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 10 }) would return { line: 1, column: 10 } offset from the range mapping, which is beyond the start of the BAee segement.

content.body: We have to cap these so that only 1 column (in this case) of source code is reverse mapped.

I think the semantics that makes sense is that ranges are inclusive of the start point but exclusive of the end point, which makes the ranges consistent because the original position mapped by the end point doesn't matter.
So `'AAAA,BAee'` maps just one point in the range. If it was `AAAA,CAee` it would map the `[0, 2)` columns
content.formatted_body: So 'AAAA,BAee' maps just one point in the range. If it was AAAA,CAee it would map the [0, 2) columns

content.body: It's arguably a little weird when the mapping spans multiple lines though, because you don't know how long any particular line is from just the source map.

* I think the semantics that makes sense is that ranges are inclusive of the start point but exclusive of the end point, which makes the ranges consistent because the original position mapped by the end point doesn't matter (and the length of the range is determined only by the generated positions).

m.new_content.body: I think the semantics that makes sense is that ranges are inclusive of the start point but exclusive of the end point, which makes the ranges consistent because the original position mapped by the end point doesn't matter (and the length of the range is determined only by the generated positions).

content.body: For this case, the range mapping doesn't have an end point so it's a bit weird. I've been thinking it might be good to make that an (optional) error case. An alternative would be to assume the range goes on until the end of the file, but I'm not sure that's intuitive.
Right now the draft spec doesn't explicitly cover that case actually, so by default it does the "until end of file" behavior

One of the goals was that for a ts-blank-space use case we'd be able to use a static source map that can be hard coded, so it'd be great to keep it as having an auto-close at the end of the file by default
This was specifically about the reverse mappings. I agree with the regular `originalPositionFor()`, the range ends when the next mapping begins.
content.formatted_body: This was specifically about the reverse mappings. I agree with the regular originalPositionFor(), the range ends when the next mapping begins.

content.body: But when going the reverse direction, how much of the generated file is covered from a ranged original mapping -> generated file
In this case, I think we can only allow 1 column of the source file to be mapped
Yeah, I think only the positions that would be reachable in the generated->original lookup should be covered in the other direction too. Is that what you mean?
Actually, let's pretend it's 2 columns, for the sake of example

So doing `generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 0 })` would fine the reverse range mapping  exactly.
`generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 1 })` would calculate an offset from the reverse range mapping.
`generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 2 })` would find the reverse range mapping, determine that the range is ended before this source point, then return `null`
content.formatted_body:
So doing generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 0 }) would fine the reverse range mapping exactly.
generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 1 }) would calculate an offset from the reverse range mapping.
generatedPositionFor({ line: 1, column: 2 }) would find the reverse range mapping, determine that the range is ended before this source point, then return null

content.body: For that last case, the GLB lookup would find the the range mapping, because it's less than the lookup point
But we can't allow it to return
Because returning `{ line: 1, column: 2 }` in the generated file would invalidate the existing `BAee` mapping in the generated file.
content.formatted_body: Because returning { line: 1, column: 2 } in the generated file would invalidate the existing BAee mapping in the generated file.

content.body: Oh I see, yes I agree it should be capped in that case.
(And that should have been `CAee`, and now `Eaee` to cover 2 columns)
content.formatted_body: (And that should have been CAee, and now Eaee to cover 2 columns)

content.body: * (And that should have been `CAee`, and now `Eaee` to cover 2 columns, VLQ is hard sometimes)
content.formatted_body: * (And that should have been CAee, and now Eaee to cover 2 columns, VLQ is hard sometimes)

m.new_content.body: (And that should have been `CAee`, and now `Eaee` to cover 2 columns, VLQ is hard sometimes)
m.new_content.formatted_body: (And that should have been CAee, and now Eaee to cover 2 columns, VLQ is hard sometimes)

content.body: That particular thing isn't speced out right now in the draft because we don't have the original to generated lookup algorithm in the spec in general

* That particular thing isn't speced out right now in the draft because we don't have the original to generated lookup algorithm in the spec in general, but the spec machinery should probably also look up the next mapping to check that the offset is in the range.

content.body: This is complicated, because we also need to verify line-wise
My array per-line works out really well for some inputs, but checking other lines becomes a pain point.
